content.text: Why Threshold-Based Alerting Fails for SLOs

The naive approach to SLO alerting is to fire when the error rate exceeds a fixed threshold, such as 1%. This fails in two ways: it fires too late for acute outages (by the time 1% of a large request volume is bad, significant budget is already consumed), and it fires too early for benign spikes (a single slow 5-minute period can briefly push the error rate above threshold without consuming meaningful budget).

Burn rate alerting solves both problems by framing alerts in terms of how fast the error budget is being consumed relative to how fast it can sustainably deplete over the SLO window. A burn rate of 1 means the budget depletes exactly at the rate that would exhaust it in the window. A burn rate of 14 means the budget is being consumed 14 times faster than sustainable.

Burn rate alerting answers: 'at the current rate, when will the budget be exhausted?' Threshold alerting answers: 'is the error rate above a number I guessed?' Only one of these questions is tied to user impact.

attrs.alt: Multi-window burn rate alert coverage — short windows catch acute outages, long windows catch sustained degradation

content.text: A complete burn rate alert suite uses multiple time windows to achieve two goals: catching acute outages quickly (where a short window is needed to detect the spike before too much budget is consumed) and catching slow, sustained degradation that a short window might miss because the signal is diluted.

The two-window requirement (both windows must exceed the threshold) dramatically reduces false positives. A single 5-minute burst of errors will fire the 5-minute window but not the 1-hour window. Requiring both prevents paging the on-call for transient blips.
Implementing Burn Rate Alerts in Prometheus

For a service with an availability SLO of 99.9% (error budget fraction = 0.001), the burn rate is computed as: error_rate_in_window / error_budget_fraction. An error rate of 1.4% yields a burn rate of 14 — the budget is being consumed 14 times faster than sustainable.

# prometheus-alerts.yaml — full multi-window burn rate alert suite
# SLO: 99.9% availability for claude-integration service
# Error budget fraction: 0.001

groups:
  - name: claude_integration_slo
    rules:

      # P0: Critical — 14x burn rate over both 5-min and 1-hour windows
      # Interpretation: at this rate, the 30-day budget is exhausted in ~50 hours.
      # The alert fires only if BOTH windows are above threshold (reduces false positives).
      - alert: ClaudeIntegrationCriticalBurn
        expr: |
          (
            job:slo_errors_per_request:ratio_rate5m{job="claude-integration"} > (14 * 0.001)
          ) and (
            job:slo_errors_per_request:ratio_rate1h{job="claude-integration"} > (14 * 0.001)
          )
        for: 2m
        labels:
          severity: critical
          team: claude-sre
          slo: claude-integration-availability
        annotations:
          summary: "Critical SLO burn: {{ $value | humanizePercentage }} error rate (14x budget consumption)"
          runbook_url: "https://runbooks.internal/claude-integration/slo-burn"

      # P1: High — 6x burn rate over 30-min and 6-hour windows
      - alert: ClaudeIntegrationHighBurn
        expr: |
          (
            job:slo_errors_per_request:ratio_rate30m{job="claude-integration"} > (6 * 0.001)
          ) and (
            job:slo_errors_per_request:ratio_rate6h{job="claude-integration"} > (6 * 0.001)
          )
        for: 5m
        labels:
          severity: warning
          team: claude-sre
          slo: claude-integration-availability
        annotations:
          summary: "High SLO burn: {{ $value | humanizePercentage }} error rate (6x budget consumption)"

      # P1: Moderate slow burn — 3x over 6h and 24h windows
      - alert: ClaudeIntegrationModerateBurn
        expr: |
          (
            job:slo_errors_per_request:ratio_rate6h{job="claude-integration"} > (3 * 0.001)
          ) and (
            job:slo_errors_per_request:ratio_rate24h{job="claude-integration"} > (3 * 0.001)
          )
        for: 10m
        labels:
          severity: warning
          team: claude-sre
          slo: claude-integration-availability
        annotations:
          summary: "Moderate SLO burn: sustained {{ $value | humanizePercentage }} error rate (3x budget)"

      # P2: Budget warning — 1.5x over 24h and 72h windows
      - alert: ClaudeIntegrationBudgetWarning
        expr: |
          (
            job:slo_errors_per_request:ratio_rate24h{job="claude-integration"} > (1.5 * 0.001)
          ) and (
            job:slo_errors_per_request:ratio_rate72h{job="claude-integration"} > (1.5 * 0.001)
          )
        for: 30m
        labels:
          severity: info
          team: claude-sre
          slo: claude-integration-availability
        annotations:
          summary: "SLO budget warning: above-sustainable error rate for 72h"

Computing burn rates in alert expressions is expensive — each alert evaluation requires multiple instant-vector computations. Recording rules pre-compute the error ratios at each window so alerts are cheap to evaluate.

# recording-rules.yaml — pre-compute error ratios for all burn rate windows
groups:
  - name: slo_recording_rules
    rules:
      - record: job:slo_errors_per_request:ratio_rate5m
        expr: |
          sum(rate(http_requests_total{status_code=~"5.."}[5m])) by (job)
          /
          sum(rate(http_requests_total{status_code!~"4.."}[5m])) by (job)

      - record: job:slo_errors_per_request:ratio_rate30m
        expr: |
          sum(rate(http_requests_total{status_code=~"5.."}[30m])) by (job)
          /
          sum(rate(http_requests_total{status_code!~"4.."}[30m])) by (job)

      - record: job:slo_errors_per_request:ratio_rate1h
        expr: |
          sum(rate(http_requests_total{status_code=~"5.."}[1h])) by (job)
          /
          sum(rate(http_requests_total{status_code!~"4.."}[1h])) by (job)

      - record: job:slo_errors_per_request:ratio_rate6h
        expr: |
          sum(rate(http_requests_total{status_code=~"5.."}[6h])) by (job)
          /
          sum(rate(http_requests_total{status_code!~"4.."}[6h])) by (job)

      - record: job:slo_errors_per_request:ratio_rate24h
        expr: |
          sum(rate(http_requests_total{status_code=~"5.."}[24h])) by (job)
          /
          sum(rate(http_requests_total{status_code!~"4.."}[24h])) by (job)

      - record: job:slo_errors_per_request:ratio_rate72h
        expr: |
          sum(rate(http_requests_total{status_code=~"5.."}[72h])) by (job)
          /
          sum(rate(http_requests_total{status_code!~"4.."}[72h])) by (job)
Each burn rate alert tier requires a different response. The goal is to match the urgency of the response to the actual budget impact — not to wake up the on-call for every P2 warning.
A P0 critical burn alert means the budget is being consumed 14 times faster than sustainable. At this rate, the 30-day budget is exhausted in approximately 50 hours. This is a paging incident.
On-call acknowledges within 5 minutes (hypercare) or 10 minutes (steady state).
Open war room if two on-calls have verified the alert is real (not a spike in a single 5-min window).
Check recent deploys: git log --oneline -10 or Argo/Spinnaker recent deploy list.
Check error distribution: are errors concentrated in one region, one model tier, one API key?
If deploy-correlated: initiate rollback. Do not wait for full diagnosis before rollback.
If not deploy-correlated: escalate to feature engineering lead and begin infrastructure investigation.
Update status page within 15 minutes of acknowledgment.
A P1 burn alert is not an immediate page in all policies — some teams use it as a high-priority ticket. The response window is 30 minutes for initial triage.
Acknowledge within 30 minutes (hypercare) or 1 hour (steady state).
Compute current budget remaining: confirm how much budget is left before the window closes.
Assess trend: is the burn rate increasing, stable, or declining? A declining rate may self-resolve.
Check for known incidents or maintenance in progress.
If the alert is sustained (>2 hours) without a known cause: escalate to P0 response.
Document findings in incident record even if no page is issued.

A P2 warning means the budget is being consumed faster than sustainable, but not at a rate that will exhaust it imminently. The response is asynchronous: review within the next business day and identify the cause.
Review alert within the next business day — this is not a page.
Identify the contributing factors: which endpoints, models, or error classes are elevated?
If no obvious cause: add a budget consumption trend chart to the weekly SRE review agenda.
If pattern is recurring: open a reliability investigation ticket.

The burn rate alerts fire when consumption is too fast, but the on-call also needs to know how much budget is left at any moment. A Grafana dashboard panel showing remaining budget and projected exhaustion time is essential for incident triage.

# Remaining error budget as a percentage of the original 30-day budget
# SLO: 99.9% → error budget fraction = 0.001

# Total budget: 0.001 × total_requests_in_window
# Consumed: error_requests_in_window
# Remaining fraction: (budget - consumed) / budget

1 - (
  sum(increase(http_requests_total{
    service="claude-integration",
    status_code=~"5.."
  }[30d]))
  /
  (
    sum(increase(http_requests_total{
      service="claude-integration",
      status_code!~"4.."
    }[30d]))
    * 0.001
  )
)

# Projected exhaustion time (hours) given current 1h burn rate
# = hours_in_window × (remaining_budget / burn_rate)
# Where: hours_in_window = 720 (30 days)

720 * (
  (1 - (
    sum(increase(http_requests_total{service="claude-integration",status_code=~"5.."}[30d]))
    /
    (sum(increase(http_requests_total{service="claude-integration",status_code!~"4.."}[30d])) * 0.001)
  ))
  /
  (
    sum(rate(http_requests_total{service="claude-integration",status_code=~"5.."}[1h]))
    /
    (sum(rate(http_requests_total{service="claude-integration",status_code!~"4.."}[1h])) * 0.001)
  )
)
A minimal error budget dashboard should include:
Hours until budget is exhausted at current 1h burn rate
Burn rate over the most recent 1-hour window
Error budget consumed per day over rolling 30d
Vertical lines at each P0/P1 incident start/end

Burn rate alerting applies to latency SLOs just as it does to availability SLOs. The latency SLI is expressed as the fraction of requests completing under a threshold (e.g., 95% under 2 seconds). The error budget is the 5% that may exceed the threshold.
A burn rate alert suite can generate too many notifications if not tuned correctly. The most common causes of alert fatigue are:

Burn rate threshold too low: a 2x threshold for the 5min+1h alert fires on transient noise. Use 14x for the P0 window — it means a 30-min outage consuming 33% of the monthly budget before it resolves.

Missing the 'for' clause: Prometheus fires immediately when the expression is true. The 'for' clause requires the condition to be true continuously for a duration before firing. Use 2m for P0, 5m for P1.
Not using recording rules: recomputing burn rate from raw counters in every evaluation cycle is slow and may time out, causing phantom alert flapping.
Single-window alerts: using only a 5-minute window will fire on every spike even if the spike is gone in 10 minutes. The two-window requirement (both must fire) is essential.
Overlapping severity levels: if P0 fires at 14x and P1 fires at 6x, a 14x event fires both. Use inhibit rules in AlertManager to suppress P1 when P0 is active for the same service.

# AlertManager inhibit rule — suppress P1 when P0 is active for same service
inhibit_rules:
  - source_match:
      severity: critical
      slo: claude-integration-availability
    target_match:
      severity: warning
      slo: claude-integration-availability
    equal: [job, slo]

Burn rate alerting converts SLO budget consumption speed into actionable alerts. The burn rate is error_rate / error_budget_fraction; a rate of 1 is sustainable over the window, a rate of 14 exhausts the budget in 1/14th of the window. A complete alert suite uses four tiers: P0 critical (14x on 5min+1h), P1 high (6x on 30min+6h), P1 moderate slow burn (3x on 6h+24h), and P2 budget warning (1.5x on 24h+72h). Two-window requirements eliminate false positives from transient spikes. Recording rules pre-compute error ratios to keep alert evaluation fast. Response playbooks must be agreed upon before the alerts go live — a P0 burn alert with no playbook is worse than no alert, because it creates confusion at the worst possible moment.
Burn rate = error_rate / error_budget_fraction. Rate of 14x = budget exhausted in 1/14th of the window.
Two-window requirement: both short and long windows must exceed the threshold before alerting — eliminates transient spike false positives.
Four tiers: P0 critical (14x, 5min+1h), P1 high (6x, 30min+6h), P1 moderate (3x, 6h+24h), P2 warning (1.5x, 24h+72h).
Recording rules pre-compute error ratios at each window; alert expressions reference the recorded metrics for efficiency.
Inhibit rules in AlertManager suppress lower-severity alerts when a higher-severity alert is active for the same service.
Budget tracking dashboard: show remaining % and projected exhaustion time — both needed during incident triage.
